title: Natural Resources Defense Council

infobox.value: * [John Adams](/wiki/John_H._Adams_(environmentalist) "John H. Adams (environmentalist)") * Richard Ayres * [John Bryson](/wiki/John_Bryson "John Bryson") * [James Gustave Speth](/wiki/James_Gustave_Speth "James Gustave Speth") * Edward Strohbehn
[New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), US

(**NRDC**) is a United States–based [501(c)(3)](/wiki/501(c)(3)_organization "501(c)(3) organization") [nonprofit](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization") international [environmental advocacy group](/wiki/Environmental_advocacy_group "Environmental advocacy group"), with its headquarters in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and offices in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Bozeman](/wiki/Bozeman,_Montana "Bozeman, Montana"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), and [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing").[[1]](#cite_note-NRDC_offices-1) The group was founded in 1970 in opposition to a [hydroelectric](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") power plant in New York.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Non-profit environmental advocacy group

Natural Resources Defense Council

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Logo of the Natural Resources Defense Council](/wiki/File:NRDC_bear_logo.svg) | |
| Abbreviation | NRDC |
| Established | 1970; 56 years ago (1970) |
| Founders | * [John Adams](/wiki/John_H._Adams_(environmentalist) "John H. Adams (environmentalist)") * Richard Ayres * [John Bryson](/wiki/John_Bryson "John Bryson") * [James Gustave Speth](/wiki/James_Gustave_Speth "James Gustave Speth") * Edward Strohbehn |
| Type | [501(c)(3) organization](/wiki/501(c)(3)_organization "501(c)(3) organization") |
| [Tax ID no.](/wiki/Taxpayer_Identification_Number "Taxpayer Identification Number") | 13-2654926 |
| Purpose | [Environmental activism](/wiki/Environmental_activism "Environmental activism") |
| Headquarters | [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), US |
| Area served | Worldwide[[1]](#cite_note-NRDC_offices-1) |
| Method | Advocacy, education, litigation |
| Membership | 2.4 million[[2]](#cite_note-2015report-2) (2015) |
| President and CEO | Manish Bapna |
| [Subsidiaries](/wiki/Subsidiary "Subsidiary") | NRDC Action Fund |
| Budget | [US$](/wiki/US$ "US$")151.6 million[[2]](#cite_note-2015report-2) (2015) |
| Staff | 700 (2020) |
| Website | [nrdc.org](https://nrdc.org/) |

The **Natural Resources Defense Council** (**NRDC**) is a United States–based [501(c)(3)](/wiki/501(c)(3)_organization "501(c)(3) organization") [nonprofit](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization") international [environmental advocacy group](/wiki/Environmental_advocacy_group "Environmental advocacy group"), with its headquarters in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and offices in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Bozeman](/wiki/Bozeman,_Montana "Bozeman, Montana"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), and [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing").[[1]](#cite_note-NRDC_offices-1) The group was founded in 1970 in opposition to a [hydroelectric](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") power plant in New York.

As of 2019, the NRDC had over three million members, with online activities nationwide, and a staff of about 700 lawyers, scientists and other policy experts.[[3]](#cite_note-fy2015financials-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natural_Resources_Defense_Council&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

NRDC was founded in 1970.[[5]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Its establishment was partially an outgrowth of the *[Scenic Hudson Preservation Conference v. Federal Power Commission](/wiki/Scenic_Hudson_Preservation_Conference_v._Federal_Power_Commission "Scenic Hudson Preservation Conference v. Federal Power Commission")*, the Storm King case.[[5]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-5) The case centered on [Con Ed](/wiki/Con_Ed "Con Ed")'s plan to build the world's largest [hydroelectric](/wiki/Hydroelectric "Hydroelectric") facility at [Storm King Mountain](/wiki/Storm_King_Mountain_(New_York) "Storm King Mountain (New York)") in New York's [Hudson Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley"). The proposed facility would have pumped vast amounts of water from the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") to a reservoir and released it through turbines to generate electricity at [peak demand](/wiki/Peak_demand "Peak demand").[[7]](#cite_note-Young-7)

A dozen concerned citizens organized the [Scenic Hudson Preservation Conference](/wiki/Scenic_Hudson_Preservation_Conference "Scenic Hudson Preservation Conference") in opposition to the project, citing its environmental impact, and the group, represented by [Whitney North Seymour Jr.](/wiki/Whitney_North_Seymour_Jr. "Whitney North Seymour Jr."), his law partner Stephen Duggan, and [David Sive](/wiki/David_Sive "David Sive"), sued the [Federal Power Commission](/wiki/Federal_Power_Commission "Federal Power Commission") and successfully achieved a ruling that groups such as Scenic Hudson and other environmentalist groups had the [standing](/wiki/Standing_(law) "Standing (law)") to challenge the FPC's administrative rulings.[[7]](#cite_note-Young-7) Realizing that continued environmentalist litigation would require a nationally organized, professionalized group of lawyers and scientists, Duggan, Seymour, and Sive obtained funding from the [Ford Foundation](/wiki/Ford_Foundation "Ford Foundation")[[5]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-5)[[7]](#cite_note-Young-7) and joined forces with [Gus Speth](/wiki/James_Gustave_Speth "James Gustave Speth") and three other recent [Yale Law School](/wiki/Yale_Law_School "Yale Law School") graduates of the class of 1969: Richard Ayres, Edward Strohbehn Jr., and [John Bryson](/wiki/John_Bryson "John Bryson").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[John H. Adams](/wiki/John_H._Adams_(environmentalist) "John H. Adams (environmentalist)") was the group's first staff member and Duggan its founding chairman;[[10]](#cite_note-10) Seymour, [Laurance Rockefeller](/wiki/Laurance_Rockefeller "Laurance Rockefeller"), and others served as members of the board.[[5]](#cite_note-Gottlieb-5)

In September 1979 The Ford Foundation pulled funding for the NRDC alongside the [Environmental Defense Fund](/wiki/Environmental_Defense_Fund "Environmental Defense Fund") after [Henry Ford II](/wiki/Henry_Ford_II "Henry Ford II") said groups receiving foundation money were "antibusiness" and "biting the hand that feeds them."[[11]](#cite_note-11) The NRDC had recently challenged the FDA's interim approval for Coca-Cola's first plastic bottle made of acrylonitrile/styrene.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The FDA reported that test animals exposed to [acrylonitrile](/wiki/Acrylonitrile "Acrylonitrile") had "significantly lowered body weight and other adverse effects, including lesions in the central nervous system and growths in the ear ducts."[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) and suspended its approval.

### Position on nuclear power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natural_Resources_Defense_Council&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Position on nuclear power")]

In the 1970s, NRDC sought to block expansion of the [Indian Point](/wiki/Indian_Point_Energy_Center "Indian Point Energy Center") nuclear power plant in New York.[[15]](#cite_note-15) It had advocated for the closure of the plant until it ceased operations in 2021.[[16]](#cite_note-16) NRDC has also sought to close the [Diablo Canyon nuclear plant](/wiki/Diablo_Canyon_Power_Plant "Diablo Canyon Power Plant") in California.[[17]](#cite_note-17) In 2018, the NRDC took no position on legislative proposals in New Jersey to subsidize three of its nuclear reactors.[[18]](#cite_note-18) NRDC has argued that nuclear power is not a viable energy source to mitigate climate change, arguing that it poses public health and safety risks through nuclear waste and nuclear proliferation.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) In 2014, NRDC president [Frances Beinecke](/wiki/Frances_Beinecke "Frances Beinecke") said that the NRDC could not support nuclear power because it would lose donations.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Position on solar power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natural_Resources_Defense_Council&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Position on solar power")]

According to NRDC, rooftop solar power plays an "essential role... in our shared mission to confront the climate crisis."[[22]](#cite_note-22) However, its activism on rooftop solar has sparked controversy. In 2022, NRDC called for reductions in subsidies for rooftop solar power in California, prompting criticism of NRDC by other environmental groups.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In 2012, NRDC sued the federal government to stop the 663.5-megawatt Calico solar station in the Mojave Desert in California. NRDC said the solar plant would imperil protected wildlife.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Position on hydropower

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natural_Resources_Defense_Council&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Position on hydropower")]

NRDC's position on hydropower is that it is not a [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") source. When Indian Point was scheduled for closure, NRDC held no position on a proposal to build a transmission line to Quebec to access excess hydropower while arguing, "we certainly would not be on board where [hydropower] gobbles up the space we think should be covered by true renewables".[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natural_Resources_Defense_Council&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Programs")]

NRDC states the purpose of its work is "safeguard the earth—its people, its plants and animals, and the natural systems on which all life depends," and to "ensure the rights of all people to the air, the water and the wild, and to prevent special interests from undermining public interests." Their stated areas of work include: "[climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), communities, energy, food, health, oceans, water, the wild".[[26]](#cite_note-26)

As a legal [advocacy group](/wiki/Advocacy_group "Advocacy group"), the NRDC works to accomplish environmental goals by operating within the legal system to reduce pollution and protect natural resources through litigation, and by working with professionals in science, law, and policy at the national and international level.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The NRDC's Center for Campaigns & Organizing (CC&O) also oversees the NRDC Action Fund, a separate [501(c)(4) nonprofit organization](/wiki/501(c)4 "501(c)4") which engages in political and electoral activities.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

NRDC published *onEarth*, a quarterly magazine that dealt with environmental challenges, through 2016. It was founded in 1979 as *The Amicus Journal*.[[29]](#cite_note-29) As *Amicus*, it won the [George Polk Award](/wiki/George_Polk_Awards "George Polk Awards") in 1983 for special interest reporting.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Staff

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natural_Resources_Defense_Council&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Staff")]

The council's first president was [John H. Adams](/wiki/John_H._Adams_(environmentalist) "John H. Adams (environmentalist)"), who served until 2006.[[31]](#cite_note-31) He was replaced by [Frances Beinecke](/wiki/Frances_Beinecke "Frances Beinecke"), who served as president from 2006 to 2015.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The third president was [Rhea Suh](/wiki/Rhea_Suh "Rhea Suh"), who served from 2015 to 2019.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In 2020, [Gina McCarthy](/wiki/Gina_McCarthy "Gina McCarthy") served as the CEO and president. She previously served as the head of the [Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency") in the Obama administration and became White House National Climate Advisor in the Biden administration in 2021.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) In 2021, NRDC selected Manish Bapna, formerly of the [World Resources Institute](/wiki/World_Resources_Institute "World Resources Institute"), as their new president and CEO.[[37]](#cite_note-37) At their web site NRDC state they have about 700 employees including scientists, lawyers, and policy advocates.

## Legislation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natural_Resources_Defense_Council&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Legislation")]

*NRDC v. U.S. EPA* (1973), with [David Schoenbrod](/wiki/David_Schoenbrod "David Schoenbrod") caused the [United States Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency") to begin reducing [tetraethyl lead](/wiki/Tetraethyllead#Phaseout_and_ban "Tetraethyllead") in gasoline sooner than they were going to.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

NRDC opposed the [Water Rights Protection Act](/wiki/Water_Rights_Protection_Act "Water Rights Protection Act"), a bill that would prevent federal agencies from requiring certain entities to relinquish their water rights to the United States in order to use [public lands](/wiki/Public_lands "Public lands").[[41]](#cite_note-3189cbo-41)[[42]](#cite_note-riversatrisk1-42)

NRDC supported the [EPS Service Parts Act of 2014 (H.R. 5057; 113th Congress)](/wiki/EPS_Service_Parts_Act_of_2014_(H.R._5057;_113th_Congress) "EPS Service Parts Act of 2014 (H.R. 5057; 113th Congress)"), a bill that would exempt certain [external power supplies](/wiki/External_power_supply "External power supply") from complying with standards set forth in a final rule published by the [United States Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy") in February 2014.[[43]](#cite_note-cbo5057-43)[[44]](#cite_note-ITIhankin-44)

## Effect on administrative law

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natural_Resources_Defense_Council&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Effect on administrative law")]

NRDC has been involved in the following [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") cases interpreting [United States administrative law](/wiki/United_States_administrative_law "United States administrative law").

* *[Vermont Yankee Nuclear Power Corp. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc.](/wiki/Vermont_Yankee_Nuclear_Power_Corp._v._Natural_Resources_Defense_Council,_Inc. "Vermont Yankee Nuclear Power Corp. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc.")*, [435](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Supreme_Court_cases,_volume_435 "List of United States Supreme Court cases, volume 435") [U.S.](/wiki/United_States_Reports "United States Reports") 519 (1978), which held that courts could not impose additional procedural requirements on administrative agencies beyond that required by the agency's organic statute or the [Administrative Procedure Act](/wiki/Administrative_Procedure_Act_(United_States) "Administrative Procedure Act (United States)").[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* *[Chevron U.S.A., Inc. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc.](/wiki/Chevron_U.S.A.,_Inc._v._Natural_Resources_Defense_Council,_Inc. "Chevron U.S.A., Inc. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc.")*, [467](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Supreme_Court_cases,_volume_467 "List of United States Supreme Court cases, volume 467") [U.S.](/wiki/United_States_Reports "United States Reports") 837 (1984), which gave administrative agencies broad discretion to interpret [statute](/wiki/Statute "Statute") to make policy changes if Congressional intent was unclear.[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* *[Baltimore Gas & Elec. Co. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc.](/wiki/Baltimore_Gas_%26_Elec._Co._v._Natural_Resources_Defense_Council,_Inc. "Baltimore Gas & Elec. Co. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc.")*, [462](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Supreme_Court_cases,_volume_462 "List of United States Supreme Court cases, volume 462") [U.S.](/wiki/United_States_Reports "United States Reports") 78 (1983)[[47]](#cite_note-BGE-Case-47) is a United States Supreme Court decision which held to be valid a [Nuclear Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Nuclear_Regulatory_Commission "Nuclear Regulatory Commission") (NRC) rule that the permanent storage of nuclear waste should be assumed to have no environmental impact during the [licensing](/wiki/Nuclear_licensing "Nuclear licensing") of [nuclear power plants](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plant "Nuclear power plant").
